coredata.dc:title: A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Zhaosheng
Chai, Lujing
Tang, Cong
Huang, Yuanyuan
Chen, Jianrong
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: This report presents a convenient and reliable fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity and inhibitor screening by taking carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal reporter. The detection mechanism is based on the following facts: (1) the aggregation of CQDs by copper (II) ions results in its fluorescence quenching due to strong coordination of copper ions to carboxyl groups of CQDs; (2) AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine into thiocholine which can induce fluorescence recovery owing to stronger affinity between thiocholine and copper ions; (3) when its inhibitor tacrine is present, AChE loses its catalytic ability for hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine, and thus the fluorescence remains quenched. With this convenient method, the activity of AChE with a concentration as low as 4.25U/L and a broad linear scope ranging from 14.2 to 121.8U/L can be assayed, which possesses adequate sensitivity for practical samples of human serum and seminal plasma. The promising application of the proposed method in AChE inhibitor screening was demonstrated. This work expands the application of carbon quantum dots in biological analysis, and provides a new fluorescent assay for AChE activity monitoring and AChE inhibitor screening using a cheaper fluorescent material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Acetylcholinesterase activity
Fluorometric assay
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Condition optimization for fluorescence quenching and recovery of CQDs
Principle of AChE activity monitoring and its inhibitor screening based on CQDs
Fluorescence quenching by copper ion and following recovery by thiol-containing compounds
Fluorescent AChE activity assay based on CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Department of Education of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405142 , 21005073 and 21275131 ) and Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B050001 , LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different composition. (a) mere CQDs; (b) in the presence of Cu2+ (23.2μM); (c) in the presence of Cu2+ (23.2μM) and Hcy (83.3μM); (d) in the presence of Cu2+ (23.2μM) and Cys (83.3μM); (e) in the presence of Cu2+ (23.2μM) and GSH (83.3μM). (B) TEM image of mere CQDs in their dispersed state. (C) TEM image of CQDs–Cu(II) aggregates. (D) TEM image of CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ and Hcy.

Changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different composition: (a) mere CQDs, (b) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM), (c) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM) and ATCh (1287.8μM), (d) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM), ATCh (1287.8μM) and AChE (278.4U/L) after 60min incubation.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the mixture containing CQDs and Cu2+ as increasing the amount of Cu2+. (B) The fluorescence intensity of CQDs versus concentration of Cu2+. (C) The influence of amount of added ATCh on the fluorescence of CQDs–Cu(II) system. (D) The fluorescence intensity of the mixture containing CQDs, Cu2+ (10.4μM), ATCh (1287.8μM) and AChE (519.9U/L) versus incubation time of mixed ATCh and AChE.

(A) Emission spectra of the assay solution containing CQDs, Cu2+ (10.4μM) and ATCh (1287.8μM) as increasing level of AChE from 0.0 to 278.4U/L. (B) Fluorescence intensity of the mixture versus level of AChE. Inset: the fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and AChE level.

(A) Changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different composition: (a) mere CQDs, (b) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM), (c) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM), ATCh (1287.8μM) and AChE (519.9U/L), (d) in the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM), ATCh (1287.8μM), AChE (519.9U/L) and tacrine (13.9μM) after 60min incubation. (B) Changes of fluorescence spectra of the assay solution as increasing amount of tacrine from 0.0 to 34.6μM.
Schematic illustration of detection strategy for AChE activity monitoring and its inhibitor screening based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly mediated by copper ions.

head.ce:title: A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This report presents a convenient and reliable fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity and inhibitor screening by taking carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal reporter. The detection mechanism is based on the following facts: (1) the aggregation of CQDs by copper (II) ions results in its fluorescence quenching due to strong coordination of copper ions to carboxyl groups of CQDs; (2) AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine into thiocholine which can induce fluorescence recovery owing to stronger affinity between thiocholine and copper ions; (3) when its inhibitor tacrine is present, AChE loses its catalytic ability for hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine, and thus the fluorescence remains quenched. With this convenient method, the activity of AChE with a concentration as low as 4.25U/L and a broad linear scope ranging from 14.2 to 121.8U/L can be assayed, which possesses adequate sensitivity for practical samples of human serum and seminal plasma. The promising application of the proposed method in AChE inhibitor screening was demonstrated. This work expands the application of carbon quantum dots in biological analysis, and provides a new fluorescent assay for AChE activity monitoring and AChE inhibitor screening using a cheaper fluorescent material.

ce:section.ce:para: As a critical enzyme in the animal's central nervous system, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) has pivotal function to maintain the levels of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine by catalyzing the hydrolysis reaction of acetylcholine (ACh) to choline [1]. It is reported that hydrolytic decomposition of ACh in the nervous system by AChE can accelerate the assembly of amyloid β peptides into amyloid fibrils and this process plays an important role in the cause of Alzheimer's disease (AD) [2]. As a result, an effective strategy is to inhibit the increasing of Ach level caused by AChE in the current AD treatment. AChE inhibitors that penetrate the blood–brain barrier have been useful in the symptomatic treatment of Alzheimer's disease and can be used as the principal drugs [3]. Therefore, in this sense, not only is AChE an important enzyme in diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease but also its inhibitors have great significance in the drug development for AD treatment. Due to the wide range fields of pharmaceutical and healthcare application, there is an urgent demand for convenient and sensitive methods for real-time AChE activity monitoring and AChE inhibitor screening.

In order to monitor the activity of AChE and to screen its potential inhibitors, various assays have been developed over the past decades including colorimetric, electrochemical, interferometric, chemiluminometric, and fluorometric approaches [4]. Among these methods, two traditional methods including colorimetric method by using Ellman's reagent [5] and the detection of hydrogen peroxide produced by oxidation of the AChE-induced choline [6–8] are widely applied in detection of AChE activity. However, the two traditional methods both have disadvantages owning to the facts that they lack sufficient sensitivity or require time-consuming procedures, and furthermore Ellman's reagent might result in false-positive effect. In order to enhance the sensitivity, many methods such as chemiluminometric or fluorometric approach based on organic compounds have been employed to establish assays of AChE activity and AChE inhibitors evaluation [9–12], which provide higher sensitivities and lower detection limits. However, dye molecules have their inherent disadvantages including rapid bleaching, insufficient emission intensities and instability. In recent years, several new fluorescent materials such as fluorescent conjugated polymers or proteins [13–15], inorganic quantum dots [16,17] and noble metal nanoparticles [18–22] have been developed and employed for AChE activity and inhibitor monitoring. Although these fluorescent materials with high fluorescence quantum yields, strong absorption and size-tunable emissions, have shown good performance in AChE activity monitoring, simple, low-cost, label-free and sensitive assays for AChE activity and inhibitor monitoring in biological samples and for discovering new drugs are still in demand.

As a kind of rising carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted intense attention of scientists in various fields due to their distinctively optical properties and outstanding performance in photovoltaic devices, photocatalysis and bioimaging [23–25]. Their outstanding advantages over organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and noble metal nanoclusters, such as stable light emitting, high quantum yield, good photostability, easy modulation, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility, endows them as an excellent fluorophore to design novel fluorescent chemo/biosensors in vitro and in vivo [26–28]. Recent research indicated that carbon quantum dots have been successfully utilized to establish fluorescent assays for several enzymes activity evaluation like protein kinase [29], hyaluronidase [30] and alkaline phosphatase [31–33].

In this study, a convenient and fluorescent assay with high sensitivity for acetylcholinesterase activity monitoring and inhibitors screening based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and hydrolysis reaction of acetylcholine to choline was developed. Carbon quantum dots abundant in carboxyl group were synthesized through chemical oxidation of cheap activated carbon, and possess strong green fluorescence. The fluorescence of CQDs was found to be quenched in the presence of copper ions (Cu2+) owing to severe aggregation of CQDs induced by the coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxyl group of CQDs. Upon addition of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) and AChE into the system, ATCh was catalytically hydrolyzed into thiocholine by AChE, and then generated thiocholine strongly bonded to copper ions from CQDs–Cu(II) nanoassembly. As a result, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered in the presence of ATCh and AChE due to re-dispersion of CQDs. By taking advantage of competitive strategy among copper ion, carboxyl group on the surface CQDs and thiocholine, and specific catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh into thiocholine by AChE, this fluorescent assay achieved high enough sensitivity for AChE level detection in practical biological sample. Moreover, AChE inhibitors screening was further assessed by means of as-established fluorescent assay using tacrine as an example. This work expands the application of carbon quantum dots in biological analysis, and provides a new fluorescent assay for AChE activity monitoring and AChE inhibitor screening using a cheaper fluorescent material.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4), acetylthiocholine (ATCh), and 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl] ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, EC 3.1.1.7, 2kU), alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1) from bovine intestinal mucosa, acid phosphatase (ACP, EC 3.1.3.2) bovine serum albumin (BSA), and immune globulin G (IgG) were bought from Sigma–Aldrich (U.S.A.). The concentration of 2-[4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl]ethanesulfonic acid buffer solution (HEPES, pH 7.4) was 10.0mM. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

The detailed procedure was reported in our previous paper [31,32]. A brief procedure is described as follows: activated carbon (2.0g) was added into a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (180mL) and nitric acid (60mL). The mixture was heated at 80°C for 5h. The mixture was cooled and diluted with deionized water (800mL). The dark CQDs solution was neutralized with sodium hydroxide. The final product solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days, and then the resultant solution was further treated with the dialysis bag (50,000Da) to remove large non-fluorescent materials. The outside solution of the latter bag was collected and concentrated for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization for fluorescence quenching and recovery of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by copper (II) ions, 3.0μL of copper (II) ion solution (10.0mM) was added into 2.5mL of CQDs solution (HEPES, pH 7.4). Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded after different incubation time of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6min respectively. Under the optimum incubation time, fluorescence quenching of CQDs was assessed with continuous addition of copper ions. The fluorescence intensity of the mixtures containing CQDs and varying amounts of Cu2+ was monitored using fluorescence spectrometer at the optimal excitation wavelength. For optimization of incubation time for catalytic hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine by AChE, 370.0μL of acetylthiocholine (10.0mM) and 15.0μL of AChE (519.9U/L) were incubated for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80min respectively, and then mixed with CQDs–Cu(II) solution, and finally its fluorescence was recorded after 15min of incubation time. For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence recovery by thiocholine generated from catalytic hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine by AChE, the fluorescence of CQDs was first quenched by adding 3.0μL of copper (II) solution (10.0mM) into 2.5mL of CQDs solution (HEPES, pH 7.4). Then, a certain amount of the mixture of ATCh and AChE which has been incubated for 1h was introduced to the above mixture. The fluorescence was recorded after different incubation time of 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 and 18min respectively.

For the AChE assay, different levels of AChE ranging from 17.4 to 278.4U/L was first incubated with 370.0μL of ATCh (10.0mM) for 60min, and then added into 2.5mL of CQDs solution (HEPES, pH 7.4) containing Cu2+ (10.4μM). After an incubation time of 15min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded respectively. Under the optimal conditions, the selectivity of the assay toward AChE was evaluated. Five biological species including bovine serum albumin (BSA), immune globulin G (IgG), acid phosphatase (ACP), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and DNA were selected to assess the selectivity of the sensing system to AChE. A certain amount of copper ion (3.0μL, 10.0mM) was first added into 2.50mL of CQDs solution. The fluorescence of the resulting mixture was regarded as the blank. Then, each of the following species including AChE (15.0μL, 100.0U/mL), BSA, ACP, IgG, ALP and DNA (15μL, 0.71mg/mL) was separately incubated with 370μL of ATCh (10.0mM) for 60min, and then mixed with CQDs–Cu solution. The resultant mixture was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy at the excitation wavelength of 452nm after 15min incubation.

For tacrine, the mixture containing ATCh (1282.5μM) and AChE (519.9U/L) was incubated for 60min with different concentrations of tacrine from 0.0μM to 34.6μM, and then the resulting mixture was added into 2.5mL of CQDs solution (HEPES, pH 7.4) containing Cu2+ (10.4μM). After an incubation time of 15min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded respectively.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemisphereical electron energy analyzer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of AChE activity monitoring and its inhibitor screening based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized using cheap activated carbon as raw materials through chemical oxidation approach according to our previous papers [31,32], because this approach not only endows carbon quantum dots strong fluorescence and functional carboxyl group on the surface, but also gives a relatively high yield and low cost of carbon quantum dots. It has been proved that carboxyl group of CQDs can serve as a good ligand for copper ion which can result in strong quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs due to their severe aggregation caused by copper ions. This aggregation caused fluorescence quenching was exploited to design the following AChE assay. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of detection strategy for AChE activity and its inhibitor screening using tacrine as an example on the basis of CQDs nanoassembly mediated by copper ion. CQDs exhibits strong yellowish green fluorescence in their dispersed state in aqueous solution, but the introduction of copper ion into CQDs dispersion leads to severe aggregation of CQDs due to high affinity of copper ion to carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs, which results in fluorescence quenching of CQDs in their aggregation state. No change can be observed in the presence of mere ATCh in CQDs–Cu(II) nanoassembly solution. However, the introduction of both ATCh and AChE into the preceding solution brings about substantial fluorescence recovery, which is achieved by the fact that thiocholine generated from ATCh under catalytic hydrolysis of AChE takes copper ions away from CQDs–Cu(II) nanoassembly due to its stronger affinity to copper ion, and this competitive binding causes breakdown of CQDs–Cu(II) nanoassembly and resets CQDs in their dispersed state. The enhanced fluorescence correlates to varying amounts of AChE level or incubation time of ATCh and AChE, and thus the assay for AChE level assessment can run in this way. When its inhibitor tacrine is added into the system, AChE activity would be inhibited with the loss of its capability to catalytically hydrolyze ATCh into thiocholine, and consequently the fluorescence of the system remains quenched due to the absence of thiocholine. The inhibition effect of tacrine on AChE activity would be reflected on fluorescence quenching degree of the standard assay.

The synthesis and characterization of CQDs were reported in our previous paper [31,32]. Briefly, CQDs were prepared by means of chemical oxidation of activated carbon powder using concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids, and further purified by dialysis which retains CQDs in size from 1kDa to 50kDa. After the preceding purification, as-obtained CQDs have a relatively narrow size distribution in the range of 2–5nm as demonstrated by TEM images in Fig. S1, and their inherent crystalline structure can also be observed by high-resolution TEM. XPS spectra in Fig. S2 indicated that CQDs are composed of carbon and oxygen in the form of CC, CO and CO, and element analysis showed their composition of 25.5at% oxygen and 74.5at% carbon. These results confirmed that a vast number of oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were generated on the surface of CQDs during the process of oxidation, which endows as-prepared CQDs excellent aqueous solubility and good complexation ability. The as-synthesized CQDs show strong green fluorescence in water under the UV lamp, and exhibit fluorescence emission band centered at 518nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 446nm (Fig. S3).

The synthesis and characterization of CQDs were reported in our previous paper [31,32]. Briefly, CQDs were prepared by means of chemical oxidation of activated carbon powder using concentrated sulfuric and nitric acids, and further purified by dialysis which retains CQDs in size from 1kDa to 50kDa. After the preceding purification, as-obtained CQDs have a relatively narrow size distribution in the range of 2–5nm as demonstrated by TEM images in Fig. S1, and their inherent crystalline structure can also be observed by high-resolution TEM. XPS spectra in Fig. S2 indicated that CQDs are composed of carbon and oxygen in the form of CC, CO and CO, and element analysis showed their composition of 25.5at% oxygen and 74.5at% carbon. These results confirmed that a vast number of oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were generated on the surface of CQDs during the process of oxidation, which endows as-prepared CQDs excellent aqueous solubility and good complexation ability. The as-synthesized CQDs show strong green fluorescence in water under the UV lamp, and exhibit fluorescence emission band centered at 518nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 446nm (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching by copper ion and following recovery by thiol-containing compounds
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve detection strategy for AChE activity by taking advantage of competitive bonding among CQDs, Cu2+ and thiocholine, we first tested general principle of fluorescence quenching by copper ion and following recovery by thiol-containing compounds. We have showed that copper ion has the strongest quenching efficiency to the fluorescence of CQDs among 13 common metal cations including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ [31], and the influence of these metal cations on the fluorescence was also displayed in Fig. S4. To verify fluorescence enhancement of thiol-containing compounds to CQDs–Cu(II) system, l-cysteine (Cys), l-homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) were chosen as examples of thiol-containing compounds because it is not accessible to obtain pure thiocholine and the detection of these biothiols is also of great interest.

In order to achieve detection strategy for AChE activity by taking advantage of competitive bonding among CQDs, Cu2+ and thiocholine, we first tested general principle of fluorescence quenching by copper ion and following recovery by thiol-containing compounds. We have showed that copper ion has the strongest quenching efficiency to the fluorescence of CQDs among 13 common metal cations including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ [31], and the influence of these metal cations on the fluorescence was also displayed in Fig. S4. To verify fluorescence enhancement of thiol-containing compounds to CQDs–Cu(II) system, l-cysteine (Cys), l-homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) were chosen as examples of thiol-containing compounds because it is not accessible to obtain pure thiocholine and the detection of these biothiols is also of great interest.

It is found that the presence of copper ion (23.2μM) leads to nearly complete quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs. By comparing the status of CQDs in the absence and presence of copper ions shown in Fig. 1B and C, we can find that dispersed CQDs with the size of 2–5nm turn into aggregated CQDs nanoassembly with the size of nearly up to 10nm after the addition of copper ions, suggesting the formation of CQDs–Cu(II) aggregates. Hence the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by copper ions is ascribed to aggregation of CQDs, i.e., aggregation caused fluorescence quenching. The following introduction of Cys, Hcy or GSH (333.3μM) into CQDs–Cu(II) system results in substantial fluorescence recovery with the best recovery ratio of up to 90% due to stronger affinity of thiol group to copper ion than carboxyl group. This competitive bonding among them is assumed to break down CQDs–Cu(II) aggregates and reset CQDs in their dispersed state. This assumption was also verified by comparing CQDs status before and after the addition of Hcy into aggregates as shown in Fig. 1C and D respectively, where aggregated CQDs turn into well-dispersed state after the addition of Hcy. The observed fluorescence enhancement by Cys, Hcy or GSH is attributed to effective disaggregation of CQDs.

To further verify the feasibility of quantitative evaluation of AChE activity based on competitive assay between CQDs, copper ions and thiocholine, we further explored to quantitate these biothiols by employment of disaggregation induced enhancement followed by aggregation caused quenching. The condition optimization including the added amount of copper ions, and the incubation times were first carried out. Results suggest the concentration of 33.3μM for copper ion and 2min incubation time as the optimal conditions. Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system for detection of Hcy, Cys and GSH were evaluated by adding different concentrations of target into the preceding CQDs/Cu2+ solution respectively. The fluorescence response of these systems was assessed by adding different concentrations of Hcy, Cys and GSH in pH 7.4 buffer solutions at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs quenched by copper ions could be gradually recovered upon the addition of different concentrations of Hcy, Cys or GSH, and linear calibration curves between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Hcy, Cys or GSH can be obtained, suggesting its feasibility of quantitative assessment of AChE activity based on competitive assay between CQDs, copper ions and thiocholine. The detection limits for Hcy, Cys or GSH are calculated as 1.45, 0.4 and 0.5μM respectively as estimated from the calibration curves (≥3 standard deviations), and their linear scopes span from 91.0 to 160.0μM. The detection limits for Cys, Hcy and GSH in this work are comparable with those based on CdTe QDs (0.1–0.6μM) [34] and those based on Ag nanoclusters (0.5μM) [35]. The quantitative assessment of biothiols based on the proposed detection strategy guarantees the practicability for quantifying AChE activity and indirectly verifies its detection strategy.

To further verify the feasibility of quantitative evaluation of AChE activity based on competitive assay between CQDs, copper ions and thiocholine, we further explored to quantitate these biothiols by employment of disaggregation induced enhancement followed by aggregation caused quenching. The condition optimization including the added amount of copper ions, and the incubation times were first carried out. Results suggest the concentration of 33.3μM for copper ion and 2min incubation time as the optimal conditions. Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system for detection of Hcy, Cys and GSH were evaluated by adding different concentrations of target into the preceding CQDs/Cu2+ solution respectively. The fluorescence response of these systems was assessed by adding different concentrations of Hcy, Cys and GSH in pH 7.4 buffer solutions at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs quenched by copper ions could be gradually recovered upon the addition of different concentrations of Hcy, Cys or GSH, and linear calibration curves between fluorescence intensity and concentration of Hcy, Cys or GSH can be obtained, suggesting its feasibility of quantitative assessment of AChE activity based on competitive assay between CQDs, copper ions and thiocholine. The detection limits for Hcy, Cys or GSH are calculated as 1.45, 0.4 and 0.5μM respectively as estimated from the calibration curves (≥3 standard deviations), and their linear scopes span from 91.0 to 160.0μM. The detection limits for Cys, Hcy and GSH in this work are comparable with those based on CdTe QDs (0.1–0.6μM) [34] and those based on Ag nanoclusters (0.5μM) [35]. The quantitative assessment of biothiols based on the proposed detection strategy guarantees the practicability for quantifying AChE activity and indirectly verifies its detection strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent AChE activity assay based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Following the design in Scheme 1, the feasibility of CQDs for qualitative detection of AChE was first assessed as shown in Fig. 2 . It is found that the presence of 10.4μM of copper ions caused a majority of fluorescence quenching of CQDs, which has been verified from Cu2+-caused aggregation. When a large quantity of ATCh was added into CQDs–Cu(II) system, no obvious change in fluorescence intensity can be observed due to negligible interaction between ATCh and Cu2+ in the aggregates. However, when the mixture of ATCh (1287.8μM) and AChE (278.4U/L) after 60min incubation was added into the preceding CQDs–Cu(II) system, a substantial fluorescence enhancement can be observed and the recovered fluorescence intensity rises to more than three times of quenched fluorescence and 70% of original intensity of CQDs, confirming the feasibility of the following quantitative evaluation of AChE level.

Detection conditions for AChE activity monitoring were optimized before quantitative evaluation was conducted. The fluorescence of CQDs was first quenched by the addition of copper ions. Hence the amount of copper ions used to quench the fluorescence of CQDs was first optimized as shown in Fig. 3A. When the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 0.0 to 23.2μM, the fluorescence of CQDs decreased continually and was almost completely quenched by 23.2μM of Cu2+. From Fig. 3B, we can see that quenching efficiency of Cu2+ amount is becoming smaller and smaller as increasing its concentration from 10.4 to 23.2μM. In addition, with consideration of time-consuming dependent on added Cu2+ amount in the following system, 10.4μM for Cu2+ was chosen as the optimal concentration for the following detection. Incubation time for fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ in Fig. S6A indicated that 1min is adequate to reach its equilibrium. The influence of ATCh amount on the fluorescence of CQDs–Cu(II) system was next examined as displayed in Fig. 3C, which illustrated that no apparent change in fluorescence intensity can be observed as increasing amount of ATCh from 0.0 to 2000.0μM, suggesting that the presence of ATCh would not exert any impact on the fluorescence. A relatively large quantity of 1278.8μM was chosen for ATCh attempting to reduce time-consumption of catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh by AChE. When such an amount of ATCh was used as the substrate concentration, time consumption for catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh by AChE can be reduced to 10min to reach a stable system as shown in Fig. 3D. Therefore, optimized detection conditions for AChE activity were chosen for the following detection according to the preceding results.

Detection conditions for AChE activity monitoring were optimized before quantitative evaluation was conducted. The fluorescence of CQDs was first quenched by the addition of copper ions. Hence the amount of copper ions used to quench the fluorescence of CQDs was first optimized as shown in Fig. 3A. When the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 0.0 to 23.2μM, the fluorescence of CQDs decreased continually and was almost completely quenched by 23.2μM of Cu2+. From Fig. 3B, we can see that quenching efficiency of Cu2+ amount is becoming smaller and smaller as increasing its concentration from 10.4 to 23.2μM. In addition, with consideration of time-consuming dependent on added Cu2+ amount in the following system, 10.4μM for Cu2+ was chosen as the optimal concentration for the following detection. Incubation time for fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ in Fig. S6A indicated that 1min is adequate to reach its equilibrium. The influence of ATCh amount on the fluorescence of CQDs–Cu(II) system was next examined as displayed in Fig. 3C, which illustrated that no apparent change in fluorescence intensity can be observed as increasing amount of ATCh from 0.0 to 2000.0μM, suggesting that the presence of ATCh would not exert any impact on the fluorescence. A relatively large quantity of 1278.8μM was chosen for ATCh attempting to reduce time-consumption of catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh by AChE. When such an amount of ATCh was used as the substrate concentration, time consumption for catalytic hydrolysis of ATCh by AChE can be reduced to 10min to reach a stable system as shown in Fig. 3D. Therefore, optimized detection conditions for AChE activity were chosen for the following detection according to the preceding results.

Under the optimal conditions, AChE activity was evaluated using CQDs and ATCh as the indicator and the substrate respectively. The dynamic response range of AChE in buffer solution was recorded through fluorescence change of CQDs. When AChE level increased from 0.0 to 278.4U/L, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was continually increasing, implying a gradual hydrolysis of ATCh into thiocholine by AChE. The plot of fluorescence response versus AChE level is displayed in Fig. 4B with a plateau point at 208.8U/L of AChE. The calibration curve in the range from 14.2 to 121.8U/L was obtained as shown in Fig. 4B inset. The regression equation for the calibration curve can be expressed as y =0.59x +141.2 with R 2 =0.99. The assay allowed the detection of AChE as low as 4.25U/L estimated from its calibration curve (S/N =3), and its detection limit is lower than those based on small organic dye (5U/L) [10] and fluorescent polymers (≥50U/L) [13,15], but inferior to those based on noble metal nanoclusters (0.05–0.1U/L) [19,20,22]. The detection limit of AChE level reported by He et al. is 2.3μU [37], but this detection limit cannot be directly compared to that in our method because different units were used and they cannot be uniformed according to the known information. On the other side, our assay possesses much wider linear scope with over 100U/L than those previous reported (less than 5U/L) [19,20,22]. The most important is that our assay possess adequate sensitivity for practical detection of AChE in human blood since it is reported that normal level of AChE in human blood is in the range of (4.29±0.66)×103 U/L [36]. To assess the specificity of the proposed fluorometric assay toward AChE, other enzymes including alkaline phosphatase (ALP), acid phosphatase (ACP), immunoglobulin G (IgG), and other biological matrix like bovine serum albumin (BSA) and DNA, were investigated as control samples. Although the concentrations of these species were much higher than that of AChE, none of them can induce apparent fluorescence response in the assay as AChE did. Fig. S7 showed selectivity factor (I − I 0)/I 0 for AChE with the level of 519.9U/L is up to 2.3 while those for the others are less than 0.1, and most of the others caused a negative response to the fluorescence. This result clearly demonstrated that this assay based on CQDs–Cu2+ system could be highly selective for the detection of AChE activity.

Under the optimal conditions, AChE activity was evaluated using CQDs and ATCh as the indicator and the substrate respectively. The dynamic response range of AChE in buffer solution was recorded through fluorescence change of CQDs. When AChE level increased from 0.0 to 278.4U/L, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was continually increasing, implying a gradual hydrolysis of ATCh into thiocholine by AChE. The plot of fluorescence response versus AChE level is displayed in Fig. 4B with a plateau point at 208.8U/L of AChE. The calibration curve in the range from 14.2 to 121.8U/L was obtained as shown in Fig. 4B inset. The regression equation for the calibration curve can be expressed as y =0.59x +141.2 with R 2 =0.99. The assay allowed the detection of AChE as low as 4.25U/L estimated from its calibration curve (S/N =3), and its detection limit is lower than those based on small organic dye (5U/L) [10] and fluorescent polymers (≥50U/L) [13,15], but inferior to those based on noble metal nanoclusters (0.05–0.1U/L) [19,20,22]. The detection limit of AChE level reported by He et al. is 2.3μU [37], but this detection limit cannot be directly compared to that in our method because different units were used and they cannot be uniformed according to the known information. On the other side, our assay possesses much wider linear scope with over 100U/L than those previous reported (less than 5U/L) [19,20,22]. The most important is that our assay possess adequate sensitivity for practical detection of AChE in human blood since it is reported that normal level of AChE in human blood is in the range of (4.29±0.66)×103 U/L [36]. To assess the specificity of the proposed fluorometric assay toward AChE, other enzymes including alkaline phosphatase (ALP), acid phosphatase (ACP), immunoglobulin G (IgG), and other biological matrix like bovine serum albumin (BSA) and DNA, were investigated as control samples. Although the concentrations of these species were much higher than that of AChE, none of them can induce apparent fluorescence response in the assay as AChE did. Fig. S7 showed selectivity factor (I − I 0)/I 0 for AChE with the level of 519.9U/L is up to 2.3 while those for the others are less than 0.1, and most of the others caused a negative response to the fluorescence. This result clearly demonstrated that this assay based on CQDs–Cu2+ system could be highly selective for the detection of AChE activity.

To further demonstrate the potential application of this assay in AChE inhibitor screening, tacrine, a well-known AChE inhibitor and a major medicine for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease, was chosen as an example to test the feasibility. As shown in Fig. 5A, the presence of Cu2+ (10.4μM) caused a most quenching of fluorescence of CQDs, and the following addition of ATCh and AChE into the preceding system lead to a substantial fluorescence recovery with 70% of recovery ratio. However, when 13.9μM of tacrine was added into the mixture of ATCh and AChE, the resultant mixture did not induce any apparent fluorescence enhancement, which clearly demonstrated the inhibition effect of tacrine on AChE. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of sensing system gradually decreased as increasing the concentration of tacrine, further confirming the correlation between inhibition efficiency of AChE and amount of tacrine. IC50 value for tacrine was calculated from the calibrated curve to be 5.6μM. These results illustrated that this established assay for AChE activity monitoring can be used for its inhibitor screening.

In summary, a new fluorometric method for AChE activity assay and its inhibitor screening was successfully developed using carbon quantum dots and acetylthiocholine as the signal reporter and the substrate respectively. Competitive coordination among copper ions, carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots and thiocholine were taken advantage of to design detection strategy of AChE through fluorescence signal switching on in combination of its catalytic hydrolysis ability to acetylthiocholine. To verify the proposed mechanism, three thiol-containing biothiols including l-cysteine, l-homocysteine and glutathione were utilized, and their sensitive detection was also achieved in this way. Although detection limit of this assay for AChE activity is inferior to those based on nobel metal nanoparticles, it possesses adequate sensitivity for AChE detection in practical samples of human blood and seminal plasma, and inherits much advantage of carbon quantum dots like low cost and toxicity. Tacrine as one of its well-known inhibitors was chosen as an example to demonstrate the promising application in inhibitor screening, and current result indicated that it is feasible to be used in inhibitor screening.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405142, 21005073 and 21275131) and Zhejiang Province (No. LY13B050001, LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parameters of cholinergic neurotransmission in the thalamus in Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease
Pharmacology of selective acetylcholinesterase inhibitors: implications for use in Alzheimer's disease
History and new developments of assays for cholinesterase activity and inhibition
A new and rapid colorimetric determination of acetylcholinesterase activity
Quantum dots-based fluorescent probes for turn-on and turn-off sensing of butyrylcholinesterase

Amperometric acetylcholine biosensor based on self-assembly of gold nanoparticles and acetylcholinesterase on the sol–gel/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/choline oxidase composite-modified platinum electrode
A fluorometric biosensor based on H2O2-sensitive nanoclusters for the detection of acetylcholine
In situ formation of metal coordination polymer: a strategy for fluorescence turn-on assay of acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening
A fluorescence “turn-on” ensemble for acetylcholinesterase activity assay and inhibitor screening
Design and synthesis of chemiluminescent probes for the detection of cholinesterase activity
Convenient and continuous fluorometric assay method for acetylcholinesterase and inhibitor screening based on the aggregation-induced emission
Continuous fluorometric assays for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibition with conjugated polyelectrolytes
Resurfaced fluorescent protein as a sensing platform for label-free detection of copper (II) ion and acetylcholinesterase activity
Novel water-soluble red-emitting poly (p-phenylenevinylene) derivative: synthesis, characterization, and fluorescent acetylcholinesterase assays
Optical detection of glucose and acetylcholine esterase inhibitors by H2O2 – sensitive CdSe/ZnS quantum dots
Analytical applications of enzymatic growth of quantum dots
Selective and sensitive detection of acetylcholinesterase activity using denatured protein-protected gold nanoclusters as a label-free probe
A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor detection based on DNA-templated copper/silver nanoclusters
A highly sensitive gold-nanoparticle-based assay for acetylcholinesterase in cerebrospinal fluid of transgenic mice with Alzheimer's disease
Continuous colorimetric assay for acetylcholinesterase and inhibitor screening with gold nanoparticles
DNA-templated silver nanoclusters for fluorescence turn-on assay of acetylcholinesterase activity
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Recent advances in bioapplications of C-dots
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Using graphene quantum dots as photoluminescent probes for protein kinase sensing
Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield and their application in sensing alkaline phosphatase
Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots – Hg (II) system
A highly selective sensor of cysteine with tunable sensitivity and detection window based on dual-emission Ag nanoclusters
Development and validation of a simple assay for the determination of cholinesterase activity in whole blood of laboratory animals
Enhanced graphene quantum dot fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive acetylcholinesterase assay and inhibitor screening

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhaosheng Qian studied chemistry in Nanjing University, China, and received his PhD in 2009. He started his academic career at Zhejiang Normal University, and promoted as associate professor in 2011. He engages in novel fluorescent sensors with carbon-based materials, and has published more than 40 scientific papers.
Lujing Chai studied at Zhejiang Normal University, and obtained her BSc in chemistry in 2012. Her research focus on the fluorescent chemosensors and biosensors based on graphene quantum dots.
Cong Tang is studying at Zhejing Normal University for his master degree in chemistry. His research focus on the fluorescent DNA probe based on graphene quantum dots.
Yuanyuan Huang is studying at Zhejing Normal University for her Master degree in chemistry. Her research focuses on the fluorescent biosensors for enzyme detection based on graphene quantum dots.
Jianrong Chen works at Zhejiang Normal University as professor majored analytical chemistry. He engages in novel fluorescent sensors for metal ion detection with carbon-based materials.

Hui Feng studied chemistry in Nanjing University, China, and received her PhD in 2009. She works at Zhejiang Normal University as an assistant professor. Her research interests focus on the development of novel biosensors targeting at DNA, proteins and peptides.
